behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: Real failure boundary is the CLI+git interaction: rename detection depends on actual repository state and git diff/rename semantics, and mocking parsed changes can hide misclassification that occurs with real git output. Unit is too low because the bug appears at module handoff (git reader -> classifier -> report). Integration was considered, but this behavior is about true rename behavior from a real repo, which is most reliably exposed by running the CLI against an actual git repo. Higher levels are disqualified by behavior text: no AI/ML model call and no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary real git repository, commit a file, rename it, run the CLI as a subprocess, parse stdout report, and assert the renamed count increments while added/deleted do not double-count that file.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: This failure often appears only with real binary content flowing through real git diff/subprocess/encoding boundaries; mocks or text fixtures can miss decode/crash paths. Unit is too low because the crash risk is at infrastructure boundaries (filesystem bytes, git output, CLI process). Higher levels are disqualified by behavior text: no AI/ML dependence or chained agentic steps.
behaviors.test_description: In a temporary real git repo, add/modify a binary file (e.g., random bytes), invoke the CLI process, assert zero crash/nonzero success status and that binary-related changes are counted in the structured stdout report.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: The critical failure boundary is handling real git behavior for an unborn HEAD/empty history in a real repo, including CLI error-path handling. A unit test of fallback logic can pass while real git command behavior still fails. Integration was considered but rejected because behavior explicitly depends on repository state as interpreted by real git/CLI execution. Higher levels are disqualified: no AI/ML output dependence, no multi-step agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a brand-new git repository with no commits, run the CLI subprocess, and assert successful execution plus an empty structured report on stdout (not an error payload/traceback).

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: This is an end-to-end correctness check over real git history semantics; failures can emerge from interactions between history retrieval, diff interpretation, aggregation, and CLI reporting. Unit tests of individual counters cannot catch mismatches introduced by real repository/query behavior. Higher levels are disqualified by text: no model-quality judgment and no multi-agent chain.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a real git repo with a scripted sequence of adds/deletes/renames/modifies across commits, compute expected totals from known operations, run the CLI subprocess, and assert exact count equality in stdout report.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: Staged vs unstaged behavior depends on real git index/worktree semantics; mocks can hide discrepancies in how the tool queries git state. Unit is too low because the failure appears when multiple modules and real repo state interact. Integration was considered but rejected since the behavior explicitly targets true staged/unstaged git behavior, best validated via real CLI+repo execution. Higher levels are disqualified: no AI/ML call and no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: In a temporary repo, create equivalent change sets once as staged and once as unstaged, run the CLI subprocess for each scenario, normalize report ordering, and assert byte-for-byte identical structured output.
